title: Plum Crumble
subtitle: Victoria plums halved and stoned under a butter and oat crumble
excerpt: Victoria plums halved and laid cut-side up in a dish with brown sugar and a little cinnamon, then covered with a butter, oat, and demerara crumble and baked until the plums are soft and jammy and the topping is crisp. The plum juices caramelise at the edges.
sourceNotes: Plum crumble follows the same structure as apple crumble but uses Victoria or other soft-flesh plums, which are at their peak in August and September in the UK. The combination of plum and warm spice is common in British seasonal cooking.

content.text: Plums release a lot of juice when baked, more than apples, and the juices caramelise at the edges of the dish into something approaching a dark, jammy sauce. This is a feature rather than a problem. Lay the plums cut-side up so they hold some of their juice as they soften rather than releasing it all at once.

Victoria plums are the standard for this recipe but any soft-flesh British plum works. Avoid cooking plums that are underripe, they will stay firm and very tart even after 40 minutes in the oven. A ripe plum yields slightly when pressed at the shoulder.

Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Lay {{plums}} of halved plums cut-side up in a 1.5-litre baking dish. Scatter {{light-brown-sugar}} of light brown sugar and {{cinnamon-ground}} of ground cinnamon over the top.

Combine {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{rolled-oats}} of rolled oats, and {{demerara-sugar}} of demerara sugar in a large bowl. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of cold butter cubes and rub in with your fingertips until the mixture is coarse and uneven with visible lumps. Scatter evenly over the plums without pressing down.

Bake for 35, 40 minutes until the topping is golden and the plum juices are bubbling darkly at the edges. Rest for 5 minutes. Serve with custard or cold cream, the contrast with the warm, jammy fruit is the point.

items.fix: Make sure the plums yield slightly when pressed before baking. If still firm after the crumble is golden, cover with foil and bake for a further 10 minutes.
items.cause: Plums were underripe, or the baking time was too short
items.symptom: Plums are still firm after baking

items.fix: Toss the plums with 1 tbsp of plain flour before arranging in the dish. This thickens the released juices.
items.cause: Very juicy plums releasing more liquid than the crumble can absorb
items.symptom: Filling is very watery and thin

items.fix: Raise to 200°C fan for the final 10 minutes. The crumble should be no deeper than 1.5 cm and should include the full amount of demerara sugar for colour.
items.cause: Oven temperature too low or topping too thick

items.fix: Add an extra tablespoon of sugar to the filling. Damsons, which are related but much sharper, need at least double the sugar of Victoria plums.
items.cause: Plums were tart varieties or underripe
items.symptom: Filling is very bitter or sharp

content.text: Plum crumble is a British seasonal dessert, tied to the short Victoria plum season in August and September. The Victoria plum was bred in Sussex in the mid-1800s. It became one of the most widely grown garden plums in Britain. Its mix of sweetness and tartness makes it well suited to cooking. The crumble, born as a wartime economy dish, remains its most common baked form.
